sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2014. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2015.assessment:
Institutional credibility declining. Pena Nieto government "Casa Blanca" corruption scandal.
El Chapo prison escape (twice) showing institutional weakness. Ayotzinapa investigation
criticized by international experts for cover-up. INE (successor to IFE) maintained
electoral credibility but other institutions under serious doubt.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2015. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2016.assessment:
Institutional functioning stable but credibility damaged. SCJN maintained independence.
INE credible for elections. Anticorruption reform passed (National Anticorruption System)
but implementation slow. Pegasus spyware revelations showed government using intelligence
tools against journalists, opposition and civil society.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2016. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2017.assessment:
Institutional framework maintained though credibility further damaged. Anticorruption
system struggling with implementation. INE credibly preparing for 2018 elections.
Federal Attorney General (PGR) widely seen as politically compromised. Judicial system
plagued by corruption and impunity.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2017. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2018.assessment:
INE credibly ran historic 2018 election that delivered AMLO landslide. Institutional
strength demonstrated by peaceful transfer of power. AMLO entered with congressional
supermajority promising institutional reform. PGR transformed to FGR (independent
attorney general) in institutional reform.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2018. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2019.assessment:
AMLO government began using congressional supermajority to weaken oversight institutions.
INE (electoral institute) under political pressure. SCJN maintained some independence.
AMLO attacked independent regulatory bodies and civil society organizations. National
Guard created to replace federal police.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2019. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2020.assessment:
Institutional erosion accelerating under AMLO. Regulatory agencies weakened or defunded.
INE budget cut. Energy reform reversed through presidential decree rather than legislation.
SCJN struck down some unconstitutional actions. AMLO consistently attacked independent
institutions. Freedom House downgraded Mexico.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2020. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2021.assessment:
Continued institutional erosion. AMLO lost supermajority in midterms but retained
majority. INE recall referendum blocked on constitutional grounds. SCJN increasingly
targeted by AMLO. Autonomous regulatory bodies systematically weakened. Freedom House
downgraded Mexico to Partly Free.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2021. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2022.assessment:
AMLO pursued electoral reform to weaken INE through street mobilization and legislation.
Massive pro-INE counter-protests by civil society blocked most damaging changes.
SCJN maintained independence blocking unconstitutional actions. INE credibly maintained
electoral oversight. Institutional resilience demonstrated.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2022. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2023.assessment:
Continued pressure on institutions in AMLO's final year. Judicial independence under
increasing attack with proposed reform to elect judges by popular vote. INE preparing
for 2024 election. Legislature used to pass constitutional changes weakening
autonomous bodies.

sources.citation: V-Dem Dataset v14, v2x_libdem, Mexico, 2023. Coppedge et al. (2024)

2024.assessment:
Judicial reform passed in September 2024 replacing judicial appointments with popular
elections — widely criticized as politicizing judiciary. US and EU ambassadors publicly
opposed reform. INE credibly ran June 2024 election giving Sheinbaum massive victory.
Morena supermajority threatens checks and balances.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Mexico

2025.assessment:
Implementation of judicial reform proceeding with first popular judicial elections
scheduled. Risk of further institutional erosion from Morena supermajority. SCJN
weakened. INE under continued political pressure. Mexico's democratic institutions
facing most serious challenge since 2000 transition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025

1995.assessment:
Zapatista (EZLN) conflict in Chiapas continued after January 1994 uprising. A ceasefire
was in effect for most of 1995 with peace negotiations ongoing. Low-level political
violence and targeted killings of activists occurred but no major armed combat.
Drug cartel violence was present but not yet at later scales.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 1995

1996.assessment:
Continued low-level Chiapas conflict. San Andres peace accords signed but not implemented.
EPR (Popular Revolutionary Army) emerged in Guerrero launching guerrilla attacks. Political
violence from drug cartels growing but not yet reaching conflict-level thresholds.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 1996

1997.assessment:
Acteal massacre (December 1997): paramilitary group killed 45 Tzotzil Maya civilians in
Chiapas, linked to PRI-affiliated groups. Continued low-level Chiapas conflict. EPR
operations in Guerrero. Political violence declining slightly from peak levels.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 1997

1998.assessment:
Political violence declining as Chiapas peace process continued despite stalled
implementation. Drug cartel violence rising in northern border states but not yet
reaching conflict-level threshold in academic datasets. Political assassinations
continued at low levels.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 1998

1999.assessment:
Political violence at relatively low levels ahead of 2000 election. Chiapas conflict
dormant but unresolved. Drug cartel violence growing in Sinaloa, Juarez and Tijuana.
Political competition generally non-violent despite intense electoral tensions.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 1999

2000.assessment:
Low political violence during democratic transition year. Fox election occurred peacefully.
No significant incidents of election-related violence. Chiapas negotiations continued.
Drug cartel violence present but not yet reaching later scales of organized violence.
Mexico's democratic transition was genuinely peaceful.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2000

2001.assessment:
Low political violence continued. EZLN Zapatista march to Mexico City and meetings with
Congress occurred peacefully. Indigenous rights bill failed but no armed escalation.
Drug cartel violence beginning to rise in border regions but not counted as political
conflict by academic standards.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2001

2002.assessment:
Political violence at historical lows. EZLN conflict essentially dormant. Drug cartel
inter-cartel violence increasing but classified as criminal rather than political violence.
Political competition conducted through democratic means. Civil society active.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2002

2003.assessment:
Very low political violence. Drug cartel violence rising especially in border cities
(Nuevo Laredo, Tijuana, Juarez) but classified as organized crime rather than political
conflict. Mexican democratic system operating without significant political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2003

2004.assessment:
Cartel violence escalating particularly in Tamaulipas (Gulf Cartel vs. Zetas) and Sinaloa.
Inter-cartel wars spreading to new territories. Political violence narrowly defined remained
low. Ciudad Juarez femicides drew international attention to violence impunity.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2004

2005.assessment:
Drug cartel violence intensifying in northern states. Nuevo Laredo saw significant
cartel-related killings including targeting of police and officials. UCDP began classifying
some Mexico cartel violence as conflict-level. Total homicides rising sharply.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2005

2006.assessment:
Calderon launched drug war with military deployment to Michoacan in December 2006.
Drug-related homicides jumped sharply. La Familia cartel rose in Michoacan. Disputed
election and Oaxaca teacher strike suppressed with federal forces (deaths occurred).
Total drug homicides estimated above 2,000. UCDP conflict classification begins.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2006

2007.assessment:
Drug war violence escalating sharply with military operations in multiple states. Cartel
fragmentation following arrests of leaders led to inter-cartel wars. Juarez and Tijuana
saw dramatic increase in killings. Total drug homicides approximately 2,700. UCDP
classified Mexico as conflict zone for first time.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2007

2008.assessment:
Drug violence doubled from 2007. Ciudad Juarez became most dangerous city in world.
Cartel battles for trafficking routes caused massive casualties. Beltran-Leyva cartel
split from Sinaloa Cartel. Army deployed across 8 states. Over 5,000 drug homicides
documented. Mexico compared to Iraq in international media.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2008

2009.assessment:
Drug violence continued escalating. Ciudad Juarez recorded over 2,600 killings in single
year. Total drug war homicides approximately 9,000. Mass graves increasingly common.
Forced disappearances at high levels. Drug cartels controlling territory in multiple
municipalities.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2009

2010.assessment:
Peak drug war violence. Over 15,000 total homicides recorded nationally with most
attributed to cartel violence. San Fernando massacres (72 migrants killed in August;
196 bodies in mass graves in March 2011). Zetas vs. Gulf Cartel split created new
violence wave. Mexico's conflict classified as high-intensity by multiple sources.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2010

2011.assessment:
Violence remained at extremely high levels though slight decline from 2010 peak. Javier
Sicilia movement for peace highlighted humanitarian cost. San Fernando massacre bodies
discovered. Zetas expanding into new territories. Movement for Peace with Justice and
Dignity brought public attention to scale of crisis.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2011

2012.assessment:
Violence began declining in some regions though Ciudad Juarez saw dramatic reduction.
Pena Nieto government took different approach — less media coverage of violence, "Mex-
ico Moment" narrative. National homicide rate began declining from Calderon-era peaks.
Some analysts attributed decline to cartel consolidation rather than government success.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2012

2013.assessment:
Violence declined significantly as Juarez crisis eased and some cartels consolidated
territory. But new hotspots emerged in Michoacan (Knights Templar) and Guerrero.
Self-defense groups (autodefensas) formed in Michoacan in response to cartel extortion.
Total homicides declining but concentrated in new regions.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2013

2014.assessment:
Ayotzinapa disappearance of 43 students (September 2014) highlighted political violence
involving state actors. Guerrero CJNG expansion. Autodefensas movement militarized.
Violence ticked upward. Tlatlaya massacre (22 killed by army, extrajudicially). UCDP
counts rose moderately.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2014

2015.assessment:
Violence began new upward trend. CJNG (Jalisco New Generation Cartel) emerged as major
actor shooting down military helicopter with rocket launcher. Guerrero and Michoacan
remained high-violence areas. El Chapo's escape renewed attention to security failings.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2015

2016.assessment:
Violence escalating sharply with CJNG expansion and Sinaloa Cartel fragmentation after
El Chapo's capture. New violence hotspots in Colima, Guerrero, and Veracruz. Total
homicides exceeded 23,000 approaching Calderon-era peaks. New cartel fragmentation
fueling territorial conflicts.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2016

2017.assessment:
Record homicide year with over 29,000 total murders. Cartel fragmentation accelerated
following El Chapo's extradition to US. CJNG expanding nationally. Violence spread to
previously peaceful states including Guanajuato, Baja California, and Quintana Roo.
Mexico surpassed Syria in total conflict deaths by some estimates.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2017

2018.assessment:
New record with over 35,000 total homicides. Mexico's murder rate at highest since records
began. Violence against candidates — 150+ politicians killed during election cycle.
Guanajuato (fuel theft conflict) emerged as new high-violence state. AMLO elected on
security platform promising transformation.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2018

2019.assessment:
Violence continued at record levels despite AMLO's "hugs not bullets" policy. Culiacan
incident (October) — government released El Chapo's son after cartel takeover of city —
seen as capitulation to cartels. Over 35,000 homicides for second consecutive year.
Huachicoleo (fuel theft) wars in Guanajuato intensifying.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2019

2020.assessment:
High violence continued despite COVID pandemic. Over 34,500 homicides. LeBaron family
massacre (November 2019) highlighted rural vulnerability. Mass graves discoveries
continued. COVID lockdowns did not reduce cartel violence. Guanajuato became Mexico's
most violent state with CJNG vs CSRL conflict.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2020

2021.assessment:
Violence maintained at high levels with approximately 33,000 total homicides. Cartel
fragmentation continued. CJNG seeking national dominance. Forced disappearances
at record levels — over 90,000 total missing persons registered.
Cartels increasingly controlling local economies through extortion.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2021

2022.assessment:
Total homicides approximately 32,000 — modest decline from peak but still historically
very high. Some evidence of cartel consolidation reducing inter-cartel fighting in some
areas. Fentanyl production by cartels drew increased US pressure. Violence against
journalists and activists continued.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2022

2023.assessment:
Modest decline in total homicides to approximately 30,000. Pre-election violence against
candidates and politicians reached unprecedented levels with 20+ candidates killed.
Ecuador's President assassination highlighted cartels' regional reach. CJNG vs. Sinaloa
Cartel continued as dominant conflict axis.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Mexico, 2023

2024.assessment:
Violence remained high with Sinaloa Cartel internal conflict (Chapitos vs. Mayos faction)
causing sharp increase in violence in Sinaloa state. Total homicides approximately 28,000-
30,000. Election-related violence against candidates highest on record. New Sheinbaum
government security approach yet to show results.

sources.citation: Reuters, Mexico violence reporting 2024

2025.assessment:
Violence trajectory uncertain. Sheinbaum government pursuing security through social
spending and intelligence rather than military. US pressure on fentanyl and cartel
designation as terrorist organizations. Sinaloa Cartel internal conflict ongoing.

sources.citation: Reuters, Mexico security reporting 2025

1995.assessment:
Mexican federal government maintained effective control over the vast majority of territory.
The Zapatista (EZLN) rebellion controlled a small autonomous zone in parts of Chiapas —
the Lacandon Jungle area — representing less than 2% of national territory. No other
significant territorial challenges to federal authority existed in 1995.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Indigenous Rights, 2003; UCDP data on Chiapas conflict

1996.assessment:
Federal government maintained broad territorial control. EZLN controlled small autonomous
communities in Chiapas with peace negotiations underway. EPR guerrilla emerged in Guerrero
and Oaxaca but did not hold territory. Drug cartels operated in border areas but had not
yet captured territory from state control.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 1997

1997.assessment:
Federal government broadly in control. Chiapas autonomous communities small. Acteal
massacre showed paramilitary presence in Chiapas but federal government retained
structural control. EPR operations limited guerrilla attacks but no territorial control.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 1998

1998.assessment:
Federal government maintained territorial control nationally. Chiapas autonomous zones
small. Drug cartel activity in border regions growing but states and municipalities
still formally under government administration. No significant ungoverned spaces outside
Chiapas autonomous communities.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 1999

1999.assessment:
Federal government in effective control of virtually all territory. Chiapas autonomous
zone small and stable. Pre-election period saw no significant territorial challenges.
Drug cartels operating primarily through corruption and infiltration rather than
territorial displacement of government.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2000

2000.assessment:
Federal government in full effective control during peaceful democratic transition year.
Chiapas autonomous communities small and non-threatening. Fox transition occurred with
no territorial challenges. Drug cartel presence in border areas but not displacing
government administration.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2001

2001.assessment:
Territorial control broadly maintained. EZLN March to Mexico City occurred peacefully,
demonstrating movement's non-violent character. Chiapas autonomous communities continued.
Drug cartels beginning to exert more influence in border municipalities but formal
government administration continued.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2002

2002.assessment:
Territorial control broadly maintained across Mexico. Some drug cartel influence in
Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Baja California municipalities but formal state structures
continued to operate. Federal institutions functioning nationally.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2003

2003.assessment:
Federal control broadly intact. Drug cartel infiltration of local and state governments
in northern border areas growing but not yet reaching territorial displacement level.
Chiapas autonomous communities small. Federal institutions operational nationwide.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2004

2004.assessment:
Territorial control maintained formally. Drug cartels increasingly challenging security
institutions in Tamaulipas and Sinaloa through violence rather than territorial control.
Municipal governments in some border areas effectively captured through corruption but
formal administration continued.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2005

2005.assessment:
Drug cartel territorial challenges increasing in Nuevo Laredo and Tamaulipas (Gulf Cartel
vs Zetas). Police stations and government buildings attacked. Some municipalities
effectively under cartel control. Federal government struggling to reassert authority in
border areas. Chiapas autonomous zone maintained.

sources.citation: Human Rights Watch, Mexico country report 2006

2006.assessment:
Calderon launched military deployment to Michoacan to challenge cartel territorial
control. Parts of Michoacan and Guerrero effectively under cartel influence. Oaxaca
had temporary contested control during teachers' strike suppression. Drug cartel
territorial displacement significant in 5-10% of territory by some estimates.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Drug War, 2007

2007.assessment:
Military deployed in Michoacan, Guerrero, Tamaulipas and other states. Cartel territorial
control growing in rural and border areas. Some municipalities in Chihuahua, Sinaloa, and
Tamaulipas effectively under cartel administration. Federal military operations temporarily
displaced cartels but control not sustained.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Drug War: The Mérida Initiative, 2010

2008.assessment:
Cartel territorial control expanding across northern states. Ciudad Juarez lost to
cartel control in many neighborhoods. Chihuahua, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas, and Guerrero
had significant areas outside effective government control. Merida Initiative launched
with US cooperation. Major cities still under government control but many rural areas not.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Drug War: The Mérida Initiative, 2010

2009.assessment:
Peak territorial challenge. Ciudad Juarez murder rate highest in world. Multiple
municipalities in Chihuahua, Tamaulipas, and Guerrero effectively under cartel administration.
Police forces fled or were captured. Possibly 15-25% of national territory had significant
cartel influence over day-to-day governance. Federal government fighting to reassert control.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Drug War: The Mérida Initiative, 2010

2010.assessment:
Territorial challenge at peak. Multiple municipalities in several states effectively
governed by cartels. Federal operations reclaiming some territory but unable to maintain
control. San Fernando massacres showed cartel control of strategic transport corridors.
UNODC estimated cartels active in 71% of municipalities.

sources.citation: UNODC, Transnational Organized Crime in Mexico, 2012

2011.assessment:
Significant territorial challenges continuing. Some improvement in Ciudad Juarez.
New areas of cartel control emerging in Veracruz, Guerrero and Coahuila. Zetas
expanding territorial reach into Central America. Federal government unable to
consistently control border regions.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Perilous Drug War, 2012

2012.assessment:
Cartel territorial challenges began declining slightly in some areas (Ciudad Juarez
improved significantly). But Michoacan and Guerrero remained heavily contested.
Pena Nieto government changed approach — military less visible, cartel capture
prioritized. CJNG began territorial expansion.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Perilous Drug War, 2012

2013.assessment:
Autodefensa (self-defense) groups formed in Michoacan in response to Knights Templar
cartel extortion, showing federal government's inability to protect communities.
Cartel control in Guerrero consolidated. Federal government negotiating with autodefensas.
Some municipalities still outside effective state control.

sources.citation: ICG, Michoacan: Paving the Way for Cartel Hegemony, 2013

2014.assessment:
Ayotzinapa disappearances showed local government complicity with cartels in Guerrero
— evidence of cartel capture rather than simple territorial displacement. Ayotzinapa
police chief worked for Guerreros Unidos cartel. Autodefensas formalized as Rural
Police in Michoacan but CJNG subsequently infiltrated them.

sources.citation: GIEI (Interdisciplinary Group of Independent Experts), Ayotzinapa Report, 2015

2015.assessment:
CJNG expanding rapidly, challenging existing cartels in multiple states. Jalisco, Colima,
and Veracruz becoming new conflict zones. Federal government unable to contain CJNG
expansion. Rural communities in Guerrero and Michoacan still outside effective state
control or under cartel administration.

sources.citation: ICG, Containing the Violence in Mexico, 2017

2016.assessment:
CJNG expansion creating new zones of contested control. Multiple states saw effective
dual authority — formal government administration alongside cartel taxation and governance.
Guanajuato, Jalisco and Veracruz experienced new territorial challenges. Rural areas
and many municipalities in 10-15 states outside effective federal control.

sources.citation: ICG, Containing the Violence in Mexico, 2017

2017.assessment:
Territorial challenges widespread across 10+ states. CJNG controlling significant
territory in western Mexico. Gulf Cartel and Zetas in northeast. Sinaloa in northwest
and mountain regions. Government effectively absent in large rural zones. Cartels
functioning as parallel governance in many communities through extortion and services.

sources.citation: ICG, Containing the Violence in Mexico, 2017

2018.assessment:
Territorial challenges at peak levels. AMLO election partly due to perception that
government had lost control. Multiple cartel fiefdoms across country. CJNG vs Sinaloa
for national dominance. Many municipalities effectively governed by cartels with formal
government present only nominally. Violence against political candidates unprecedented.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Everyday War: Guerrero and the Failures of Peace, 2018

2019.assessment:
AMLO's Culiacan incident (October 2019) — government released cartel leader's son after
cartel takeover of city — demonstrated cartel capacity to contest government control even
in major cities. Significant areas of Guerrero, Michoacan, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas with
minimal effective state presence.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Everyday War: Guerrero and the Failures of Peace, 2018

2020.assessment:
Territorial control situation broadly unchanged from 2019. Cartels expanded presence
during COVID as government capacity reduced. CJNG expansion continued. Many communities
in Guerrero, Michoacan, Chiapas, Tamaulipas and other states under cartel governance.
AMLO pursued negotiated peace with cartels rather than military confrontation.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Everyday War, 2020

2021.assessment:
CJNG and Sinaloa Cartel dominant with territorial bases. Many municipalities in 15+
states with significant cartel governance. Guerrero effectively a failed state at
municipal level in many areas. AMLO's "hugs not bullets" policy unable to restore
government presence in cartel-held territories.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Spiraling Internal Conflict, 2021

2022.assessment:
Territorial control situation broadly persistent with extensive cartel governance in
significant portion of Mexico. CJNG expanding into new states. Some cartel consolidation
reducing inter-cartel violence in stabilized territories. State presence nominal in
many municipalities of violence-affected states.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Spiraling Internal Conflict, 2022

2023.assessment:
Territorial control challenges maintained. Cartel governance well-established in
Guerrero, Michoacan, Tamaulipas, Zacatecas, and other states. Sheinbaum (governor
of CDMX) preparing federal transition. Violence against 2024 election candidates
showed cartel territorial assertion in electoral context.

sources.citation: ICG, Mexico's Spiraling Internal Conflict, 2023

2024.assessment:
Sinaloa Cartel internal civil war (Chapitos vs. Mayo Zambada faction) created new
territorial instability in Sinaloa, Sonora and Baja California. Sheinbaum administration
reviewing security strategy. Significant portions of 10+ states still with minimal
effective government presence.

sources.citation: Reuters, Mexico security reporting 2024

2025.assessment:
Territorial control situation unclear with Sinaloa Cartel reconfiguration underway.
US "terrorist organization" designation of cartels may affect security cooperation.
Sheinbaum pursuing social-led security approach. Multiple states remain with
significant cartel governance in rural and peri-urban areas.

sources.citation: Reuters, Mexico security reporting 2025

1995.assessment:
IBP Open Budget Survey did not begin until 2006. For the PRI-era, Mexico's budget
process was highly opaque and controlled by the executive. The President (PRI)
had near-unilateral control over the federal budget. Congress had minimal oversight
role and budgets were rarely scrutinized publicly. PEMEX revenues and off-budget
expenditures obscured fiscal transparency. No meaningful citizen access to budget
documents. Estimated score consistent with "minimal" transparency category.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment; World Bank Public Financial Management reviews

1996.assessment:
PRI-era budget opacity continuing. Executive domination of budget process.
Post-Tequila crisis period involved some increase in fiscal disclosure requirements
from IMF/World Bank as conditions of support — but public transparency remained
limited. Congressional oversight still largely nominal under PRI single-party rule.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment; IMF Article IV Consultations Mexico 1996

1997.assessment:
Mid-term elections in 1997 resulted in PRI losing its absolute majority in the
Chamber of Deputies for the first time — opposition won control. This began a
significant shift in budget transparency as Congress now had incentives to scrutinize
executive budget proposals. The 1997 election represented the beginning of budget
oversight improvement, though change was gradual.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment; academic literature on Mexican budgetary reform

1998.assessment:
Congressional opposition using new budget oversight authority. Debates over fiscal
transparency following Tequila crisis. Mexico under IMF surveillance increasing
fiscal reporting. Some early progress on publishing budget documents though still
limited compared to OECD standards. FOBAPROA bank bailout scandal raised public
interest in fiscal accountability.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment; FOBAPROA parliamentary debates

1999.assessment:
Pre-election year with growing demand for transparency. Congress continuing to
assert budget oversight. Mexico's fiscal position strengthening. Some international
transparency norms beginning to be adopted. Still well below OECD norms but
improving trajectory as political competition increased accountability demands.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment

2000.assessment:
Fox victory and democratic transition marked turning point for budget transparency.
The new government made budget openness a stated priority. The 2000-2001 period
saw increased publication of budget documents and the creation of the ASF
(Superior Auditor of the Federation). Congress (opposition-controlled) pressing
for greater transparency. IBP survey not yet conducted but trajectory clearly
improving.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Initiative early reports; Fox government transparency commitments

2001.assessment:
Fox government pursuing transparency agenda. 2001 saw early efforts at improved
budget publication. ASF established and beginning audit work. Federal Transparency
and Access to Public Government Information Law under discussion (passed 2002).
Congressional budget oversight strengthening. Estimated progress toward limited
transparency range.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment; Fox government transparency reforms

2002.assessment:
Federal Law of Transparency and Access to Public Government Information (LFTAIPG)
passed 2002 — landmark transparency legislation. Created IFAI (Federal Institute
for Access to Information). Budget documents increasingly accessible online.
Congressional budget oversight improving. First FOIA-based access to government
information in Mexico. Significant step toward open budget practices.

sources.citation: LFTAIPG 2002 (Federal Law of Transparency); IBP assessment

2003.assessment:
IFAI operating, implementing transparency law. Budget documents increasingly
published online. ASF conducting audits and publishing results. Some resistance
from agencies to transparency requirements. Congress continuing oversight role.
Improvement continuing toward IBP scoring baseline.

sources.citation: IFAI annual report 2003; ASF audit reports

2004.assessment:
Budget transparency improving. IFAI functioning. Budget documents increasingly
available. Executive budget proposal, enacted budget, and audit reports published.
However, significant gaps remained: mid-year reporting weak, supplementary budgets
opaque, citizen budget not produced. Score approaching IBP's 2006 first measurement
of 52.

sources.citation: IBP historical assessment; World Bank Mexico PER

2005.assessment:
Pre-IBP survey year. Budget transparency at approximately IBP "limited" category.
Fox administration publishing more budget documents. ASF audits more rigorous.
Congressional oversight functioning but executive still dominant. Oil revenues
(PEMEX) partially off-budget, limiting full transparency. 2006 IBP score of 52
reflects this period.

sources.citation: IBP, Open Budget Survey 2006 (Mexico score: 52)

2006.assessment:
First IBP Open Budget Survey measurement for Mexico: score of 52 — "limited"
transparency category. Mexico provided some budget information publicly but
had significant gaps in mid-year reporting, citizens budget, and pre-budget
statement. Executive budget proposal and enacted budget available. ASF audits
published. PEMEX oil revenues remained somewhat opaque.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2006, Mexico

2007.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained at similar level between IBP surveys. Calderon
government (took office December 2006) continuing with transparency framework
inherited from Fox. IFAI functioning. Some deterioration in oil revenue transparency
as PEMEX fiscal pressures increased due to declining production.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation; IFAI annual report 2007

2008.assessment:
IBP OBS 2008: Mexico scored 52 — unchanged from 2006. Limited budget transparency
category. While executive budget proposal and enacted budget were publicly available,
citizens budget was absent, mid-year reports were weak, and year-end report quality
was limited. PEMEX oil revenues and transfers to federal government not fully transparent.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2008, Mexico

2009.assessment:
Estimated improving budget transparency. Global financial crisis prompted some
additional fiscal reporting to markets and multilateral organizations. Mexico's
stimulus package for 2009 crisis required budget justification. Trajectory upward
toward 2010 IBP score of 64.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation between 2008 and 2010 scores

2010.assessment:
Significant improvement — IBP OBS 2010: Mexico scored 64, up from 52 in 2008.
Now in "substantial" transparency category. Mexico introduced citizens budget and
improved mid-year reporting. SHCP (Finance Ministry) expanded online budget
portal. New General Government Accounting Law (2008) being implemented.
Move to accrual accounting improving quality. Still gaps in supplementary budget
transparency.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2010, Mexico

2011.assessment:
Budget transparency stable in substantial range. Continued implementation of
General Accounting Law. SHCP budget portal maintained. Some backsliding possible
in supplementary budget processes. Drug war costs and off-budget security spending
creating some transparency gaps.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation between 2010 and 2012 scores

2012.assessment:
IBP OBS 2012: Mexico scored 61 — slight decline from 2010 but still substantial
category. Pre-budget statement and citizens budget available. Some deterioration
noted in quality of in-year reports. Pena Nieto won election 2012 — transition
year possibly affecting publication timelines. Overall still above OECD average
for developing country.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2012, Mexico

2013.assessment:
Budget transparency improving under Pena Nieto reform agenda. Pacto por Mexico
included transparency commitments. National Anti-Corruption System discussions
underway. Fiscal Transparency portal (transparenciapresupuestaria.gob.mx) being
enhanced. PEMEX transparency improving as part of energy reform preparations.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation; Mexican Finance Ministry transparency reports

2014.assessment:
Continued improvement trajectory. Energy reform implementation required enhanced
fiscal transparency around PEMEX and new energy contracts. Ayotzinapa scandal
triggered some examination of state-level budget opacity. Federal budget
transparency improving while state/municipal transparency highly variable.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation; SHCP transparencia presupuestaria portal

2015.assessment:
IBP OBS 2015: Mexico scored 66 — in substantial transparency range. Citizens
budget produced, pre-budget statement available, mid-year reporting improved.
Transparent budgeting portal widely recognized. However oil price collapse
complicated PEMEX-related fiscal transparency. National Anti-Corruption System
constitutional amendment passed. Overall one of better performers in Latin America.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2015, Mexico

2016.assessment:
Budget transparency likely declining somewhat. Oil price decline hitting PEMEX
revenues causing complex mid-year budget adjustments. Austerity measures
complicating budget process transparency. National Anti-Corruption System
constitutional amendment passed 2016 but institutions slow to form.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation between 2015 and 2017 scores

2017.assessment:
IBP OBS 2017: Mexico scored 58 — decline to lower substantial / upper limited
range. IBP cited regression in quality of in-year reports, weakening of citizens
budget. Fiscal austerity and oil sector difficulties affecting budget publication
quality. PEMEX financial crisis creating off-budget pressures. Despite institutional
improvements, practical budget transparency declined from 2015 peak.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2017, Mexico

2018.assessment:
Budget transparency improving toward end of Pena Nieto administration and through
AMLO transition. AMLO's platform emphasized anti-corruption and transparency.
NAIM airport cancellation (October 2018) via citizen consultation showed new
approach to spending accountability. Trajectory toward 2019 IBP score of 76.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation between 2017 and 2019 scores

2019.assessment:
IBP OBS 2019: Mexico scored 76 — substantial transparency, near extensive category.
Significant improvement. AMLO government made budget transparency a stated priority.
Enhanced publication of budget documents. Citizens budget improved. Mid-year and
year-end reports stronger. Mexico among top performers in Latin America. However
AMLO's direct spending programs (welfare programs run by military) raised some
concerns about off-budget activities.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2019, Mexico

2020.assessment:
Budget transparency maintained at high level. COVID emergency spending required
supplementary budgets that created some transparency challenges. AMLO's use of
military for infrastructure projects and some off-budget fund transfers raised
concerns. Core budget documentation remained strong.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation between 2019 and 2021 scores

2021.assessment:
IBP OBS 2021: Mexico scored 75 — substantial transparency, slight decline from 76.
Overall strong budget transparency maintained. Concerns about transparency of
AMLO government's large infrastructure projects (Tren Maya, Dos Bocas refinery)
managed through trust funds outside normal procurement. Military-managed programs
reducing some transparency. Core federal budget documents published publicly.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2021, Mexico

2022.assessment:
Budget transparency broadly maintained at substantial level. AMLO government
continuing use of military-managed trust funds for infrastructure projects,
reducing oversight. PEMEX rescue spending increasing off-budget pressures.
Core federal budget documents published. Trend toward slight decline.

sources.citation: IBP interpolation between 2021 and 2023 scores

2023.assessment:
IBP OBS 2023: Mexico scored 73 — substantial transparency category. Slight
continuing decline from 76 peak in 2019. Military-managed budgets and trust
fund opacity remain concerns. Overall core federal budget documentation strong.
Mexico remains among best performers in Latin America on budget transparency
despite some erosion. Sheinbaum government transition year.

sources.citation: International Budget Partnership, Open Budget Survey 2023, Mexico

2024.assessment:
Budget transparency likely broadly maintained under Sheinbaum government.
Sheinbaum positioned as more institutionally orthodox than AMLO on governance.
Some continuation of AMLO-era military/trust fund structures. Next IBP survey
(2025) not yet published. Estimated at approximately 2023 level.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2023 as most recent data point; trend extrapolation

2025.assessment:
Budget transparency data for 2025 not yet available. IBP conducts surveys every
two years; next survey after 2023 would cover approximately 2025. Sheinbaum
government broadly maintaining institutional frameworks. No major transparency
regression expected but also no major improvement signaled.

sources.citation: IBP anticipated 2025 survey; trend extrapolation

1995.assessment:
WJP Index not yet available. In the PRI era, legal transparency was severely limited.
Laws were formally published in the Diario Oficial but interpretation was politically
determined. Regulations were applied selectively and arbitrarily. Access to justice
was restricted by informal payments and connections. The executive dominated the
judiciary — Supreme Court justices served at the pleasure of the president.
Citizens had limited ability to access, understand, or invoke legal protections.
Estimated as difficult/minimal rule of law transparency.

sources.citation: Academic literature on PRI-era rule of law: Magaloni (2003), Domingo (2000)

1996.assessment:
PRI-era legal opacity continuing. Laws published in Diario Oficial but regulatory
framework complex and opaque. Arbitrary enforcement common. No freedom of
information law. Judiciary subordinate to executive. Rule of law weak.
Business contracts often enforced through political connections rather than
legal system.

sources.citation: Academic literature; World Bank Doing Business historical estimates

1997.assessment:
1994 Zedillo judiciary reform began making Supreme Court more independent.
Congressional elections 1997 reduced executive dominance. Some early improvement
in legal transparency trajectory. However, rule of law in practice still
severely limited by endemic corruption and political interference.

sources.citation: Domingo (2000), Judicial Independence in Latin America; academic

1998.assessment:
Gradual improvement in formal legal framework. FOBAPROA banking scandal raised
demand for clearer legal accountability. Supreme Court beginning to assert
independence from executive. However, practical legal transparency for citizens
still highly limited. Access to information formally restricted.

sources.citation: Academic assessment; World Bank rule of law indices (WGI)

1999.assessment:
Gradual improvement continuing. Pre-democratic transition with growing pressure
for legal transparency. Opposition parties in Congress pressuring for clearer
laws. Judiciary gaining some independence. Still well below what would be expected
of a democratic rule-of-law state.

sources.citation: Estimated; World Bank Governance Indicators historical

2000.assessment:
Democratic transition brought new emphasis on legal transparency. Fox government
committed to FOIA legislation, judicial independence, and equal application of law.
Some improvement in legal framework accessibility. Supreme Court more visibly
independent. However, deep cultural and structural barriers to legal transparency
persisted — corruption, impunity, selective enforcement.

sources.citation: Fox government transparency commitments; World Bank Governance Indicators

2001.assessment:
Fox government transparency agenda advancing. FOIA law being drafted. Federal
judiciary reform continuing. Amparo litigation (constitutional challenge mechanism)
increasingly accessible. Some improvement in publication and accessibility of laws
and regulations. Private sector law firms expanding and improving legal information
access.

sources.citation: Estimated; Fox government reform agenda

2002.assessment:
Federal Transparency and Access to Public Government Information Law (LFTAIPG)
passed 2002 — landmark for legal transparency. Citizens now had formal right to
access government information including laws, regulations, contracts, and procedures.
IFAI created to enforce access rights. Significant improvement in legal framework
accessibility. However, enforcement uneven and state/municipal level lagged.

sources.citation: LFTAIPG 2002; IFAI creation; academic assessments

2003.assessment:
IFAI implementing LFTAIPG. Legal information becoming more accessible. Federal
courts publishing jurisprudencia (case law) online. Regulatory framework still
complex but improving. State-level transparency laws beginning to pass in some
states. Structural barriers (corruption, impunity) still limiting practical
legal transparency.

sources.citation: IFAI first year reports; SCJN jurisprudence portal

2004.assessment:
Legal transparency gradually improving. Federal laws and regulations accessible
online. SCJN (Supreme Court) publishing decisions and jurisprudence. IFAI
operating and resolving information access disputes. State-level variation
significant — some states highly opaque. World Bank Doing Business indicators
show moderate performance. Approaching WJP measurement period.

sources.citation: Estimated; World Bank Doing Business 2004 Mexico

2005.assessment:
Legal transparency broadly in partial/significant gaps range. Federal laws
accessible. SCJN decisions published. IFAI operating. However, judicial system
still plagued by corruption, backlog, and unequal access. Regulatory complexity
remains a barrier. State-level legal frameworks highly variable.

sources.citation: Estimated; approaching WJP first surveys

2006.assessment:
Legal transparency improving but contested election 2006 (Calderon vs Lopez Obrador)
raised concerns about institutional legitimacy. Federal Electoral Tribunal ruling
validated Calderon victory. IFAI increasingly effective. INEGI Law passed improving
data accessibility. Drug war impacts on rule of law beginning.

sources.citation: Estimated; IFAI reports; Calderon post-election period

2007.assessment:
Legal transparency in partial/improving range. Federal level laws and court
decisions increasingly accessible online. Drug war creating extrajudicial
dynamics undermining rule of law in some regions — military courts applied to
drug war crimes. Calderon government constitutional reform discussions.

sources.citation: Estimated; World Bank WGI Rule of Law Mexico 2007

2008.assessment:
Major constitutional reform of the criminal justice system in 2008 — transition
from inquisitorial to oral/accusatory system. This was a landmark legal transparency
improvement — court proceedings now public, oral, and adversarial rather than
written and secret. However, implementation took 8 years (completed 2016).
Reform represented significant improvement in legal accessibility when
fully implemented.

sources.citation: Constitutional Reform 2008 — criminal justice system overhaul

2009.assessment:
Legal transparency broadly stable. Criminal justice reform being slowly
implemented in some states. Drug war undermining rule of law in conflict zones —
evidence of cartel capture of local courts and prosecutors in some regions.
Federal courts more independent. World Bank Doing Business 2009 showed moderate
performance.

sources.citation: Estimated; World Bank WGI

2010.assessment:
Legal transparency at partial-to-moderate level. Criminal justice reform
implementation ongoing in some states. IFAI strengthened. Constitutional
reforms improving legal framework. Drug war and cartel capture of local
governments in some regions severely undermining practical rule of law.
WJP pilot surveys in this period estimated Mexico around 0.40 equivalent.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index pilot surveys; World Bank WGI

2011.assessment:
WJP Rule of Law Index first full measurement: Factor 3 (Open Government) for Mexico
approximately 0.41. Partial legal transparency. Laws accessible federally but
subnational variation extreme. IFAI functioning. Some regulations opaque or
selectively enforced. Criminal justice reform mid-implementation. Drug war
undermining rule of law in conflict regions. Mexico ranked mid-tier globally.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2011, Mexico, Factor 3 Open Government

2012.assessment:
WJP 2012: Mexico Factor 3 approximately 0.41. Broadly stable. Criminal justice
reform continuing implementation. IFAI increasing effectiveness. Pena Nieto
election with transparency commitments. Significant subnational variation —
some states more transparent than others. Drug war continuing to undermine
practical rule of law in affected regions.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2012, Mexico

2013.assessment:
WJP 2013: slight improvement. Pena Nieto's Pacto por Mexico included legal
transparency commitments. Telecom reform improving information access.
National Anti-Corruption System discussions. Criminal justice reform progressing.
Information access law improvements. However, endemic corruption and impunity
still severely limiting practical legal transparency.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2013, Mexico

2014.assessment:
WJP 2014: stable at approximately 0.42. Ayotzinapa disappearances exposed
profound legal opacity — state and local officials complicit in murders,
evidence destroyed, impunity complete. This high-profile case revealed gap
between formal legal framework and practical rule of law. National Anti-Corruption
System constitutional amendment pending.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2014, Mexico

2015.assessment:
WJP 2015: approximately 0.43. National Anti-Corruption System constitutional
reform passed. Criminal justice reform 8-year implementation approaching deadline
(completed June 2016). New General Law of Transparency passed 2015 expanding
and harmonizing FOIA framework. Overall improving legal transparency framework
even as practical implementation lagged.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2015, Mexico

2016.assessment:
WJP 2016: approximately 0.43. Criminal justice reform fully implemented nationally
(oral/adversarial system) — all states transitioned by June 2016. This was a major
legal transparency milestone. National Anti-Corruption System institutions being
established (SNAS, SNF, SEA). INAI (upgraded IFAI) with broader mandate.
Practical impact of reforms limited by corruption and implementation gaps.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2016, Mexico

2017.assessment:
WJP 2017-18: Mexico approximately 0.43. Anti-corruption system formal but
implementation slow — Attorney General (FGR) delayed appointment, INAI
functioning. Criminal justice system adjusting to new oral procedures. Courts
more transparent with public hearings. Practical legal transparency still
undermined by corruption, political interference, cartel influence over local
courts.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2017-2018, Mexico

2018.assessment:
WJP 2018: approximately 0.43. AMLO won election in July 2018 on anti-corruption
platform. Some concerns raised about AMLO's use of presidential decrees and
non-standard regulatory approaches. National Anti-Corruption System still not
fully staffed. FGR (Fiscalia) becoming more independent but politically complex.
Overall legal transparency framework stable though undermined by persistent
impunity.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2018, Mexico

2019.assessment:
WJP 2019: approximately 0.43 (slight variation). AMLO government pursuing rule
by decree for major policy changes (infrastructure projects bypassing regulatory
review). NAIM cancellation via improvised referendum raised legal concerns.
INAI under some pressure. Practical legal transparency deteriorating in some
areas despite formal framework maintenance.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2019, Mexico

2020.assessment:
WJP 2020: approximately 0.41 — slight decline. AMLO government bypassing
regulatory frameworks for infrastructure projects (Tren Maya, Dos Bocas refinery)
using military and trust funds outside procurement laws. COVID emergency decrees
bypassed normal regulatory process. INAI under increasing government pressure.
Practical deterioration in rule of law transparency despite formal institutions
persisting.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2020, Mexico

2021.assessment:
WJP 2021: approximately 0.41. AMLO government weakening INAI (information access
institute) and other transparency institutions through budget cuts and public
criticism. Supreme Court challenging some AMLO executive actions — sign of
some judicial independence. Electricity reform by presidential decree raised
constitutional questions. Legal transparency framework stressed.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2021, Mexico

2022.assessment:
WJP 2022: approximately 0.40. Legal transparency declining under AMLO. INAI under
severe pressure — AMLO publicly criticizing INAI, proposing abolition in some
statements. Judicial review of AMLO's actions increasing with Supreme Court blocking
several initiatives. Military-managed infrastructure projects outside normal legal
oversight. Significant deterioration from pre-AMLO levels.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2022, Mexico

2023.assessment:
WJP 2023: approximately 0.40. AMLO continued attacking judicial independence.
Proposed judicial reform (elected judges replacing appointed system) raised major
rule of law concerns. INAI non-functional for some months due to vacancy in
commissioners — access to government information blocked. Pre-Sheinbaum transition
year. Mexico declining in WJP global rankings.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2023, Mexico

2024.assessment:
WJP 2024: approximately 0.38. Judicial reform passed (judges now elected by popular
vote) — major change to judiciary. International criticism (US, Canada, UN, bar
associations) that elected judges undermine rule of law. INAI abolished and folded
into a new body with different powers under Sheinbaum government.
Legal transparency significantly declining. Mexico dropped substantially in WJP rankings.

sources.citation: WJP, Rule of Law Index 2024, Mexico; judicial reform analysis

2025.assessment:
Legal transparency continuing to deteriorate following 2024 judicial reform.
First elections for judges under new system scheduled for June 2025. Profound
uncertainty about judicial independence and rule of law. INAI replacement
body untested. WJP 2025 index not yet published. Trajectory clearly negative
from transparency perspective.

sources.citation: Academic and media analysis of judicial reform 2024-2025; WJP not yet published

1995.assessment:
RSF Index did not begin until 2002. In the PRI era (pre-2000), Mexico had significant
press constraints. Major newspapers (Excelsior, Novedades) received government advertising
(PIPSA newsprint monopoly) creating systemic self-censorship. Journalists critical of
government or covering drug trafficking faced harassment, violence, and job loss.
However, some independent investigative journalism existed, especially in capital.
Estimated as "very serious" conditions based on Freedom House and academic assessments
of PRI-era media.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom of the Press historical reports; Lawson (2002) Building the Fourth Estate

1996.assessment:
PRI-era press constraints continuing. PIPSA state newsprint monopoly still
operating (abolished 2001). Drug cartel violence against journalists beginning
to emerge — reporters covering Gulf Cartel in Tamaulipas and Sinaloa Cartel
facing threats. Some liberalization in political coverage as opposition newspapers
gained readership. Proceso and Reforma newspapers providing more independent
coverage.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom of the Press 1996-1997

1997.assessment:
Some improvement. PRI lost Chamber majority in 1997 elections, reducing executive
control over political discourse. Independent media (Reforma, La Jornada) becoming
stronger. Government advertising still used as political tool but weakening. Drug
cartel threats to journalists in northern border states growing. Estimated slight
improvement from PRI dominance period.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom of the Press 1997-1998

1998.assessment:
Continued gradual improvement in political press freedom as competition increased.
Drug war-related threats to journalists in border areas growing. Several reporters
covering drug trafficking disappeared or were murdered. PIPSA newsprint monopoly
still operating but government advertising as censorship tool declining. Online
media beginning to emerge.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom of the Press 1998-1999; CPJ Mexico reports

1999.assessment:
Pre-election year with growing media pluralism. Competition among parties requiring
media coverage of opposition. Government still using advertising as political tool
but more constrained. Drug-related journalist killings increasing. CPJ documented
increasing harassment of journalists covering cartels. Estimated trajectory toward
partial improvement around 2000 democratic transition.

sources.citation: CPJ, Attacks on the Press 1999; Freedom House

2000.assessment:
Democratic transition brought improvement in political press freedom. Fox committed
to press freedom and abolition of PIPSA newsprint monopoly. Government advertising
less systematically used as censorship tool. Opposition media able to operate.
However, drug cartel violence against journalists continued and grew. CPJ documented
multiple journalist killings in Mexico in 2000 related to organized crime.
Problematic/difficult transition period.

sources.citation: CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2000; Freedom House Freedom of the Press 2001

2001.assessment:
Fox government abolished PIPSA newsprint monopoly in 2001 — major reform
removing key government censorship tool. Freedom of Information law in
discussion. Political press more free than under PRI. Drug cartel threats to
journalists continuing. CPJ documented 3 journalists killed in Mexico in 2001.
RSF index not yet available; approaching first measurement year.

sources.citation: CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2001; Fox government PIPSA abolition

2002.assessment:
First RSF index year: Mexico ranked around position 75-80 globally with score
approximately 52 (on RSF raw scale, lower=better pre-2013; converted to 0-100
inverted). Political press relatively free post-transition but drug-related
journalist violence serious and growing. Mexico becoming one of the most dangerous
countries for journalists globally due to organized crime, not state repression.
CPJ documented 3 journalist killings in 2002.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2002; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2002

2003.assessment:
Press freedom conditions broadly similar. Transparency law (IFAI) operating,
improving access to official information. Drug-related journalist killings
continuing. Mexican reporters covering cartel activity in Tamaulipas, Sinaloa,
Michoacan facing severe threats. Self-censorship on drug coverage widespread
in affected regions. Political coverage more free.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2003; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2003

2004.assessment:
Press freedom conditions stable but deteriorating in drug war zones. CPJ documented
multiple journalist killings and disappearances in border states. Self-censorship
widespread in drug conflict areas — local newspapers in Tamaulipas, Nuevo Laredo
refusing to cover cartel activity. Political coverage at federal level remains
reasonably free.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2004; CPJ Mexico country page

2005.assessment:
Conditions worsening in drug war zones. Multiple journalists killed in Nuevo Laredo
region. RSF and CPJ increasingly citing Mexico as one of most dangerous countries
for journalists. Self-censorship in drug conflict areas near total — no local media
covering cartel activities in most dangerous municipalities. National and capital
press still relatively free.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2005; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2005

2006.assessment:
Calderon government launched military drug war in December 2006 — military-cartel
conflict now directly impacting journalists. Some reporters embedded with military
but independent investigative journalism highly dangerous. Political coverage
of 2006 contested election relatively free. RSF ranking around 75-90.
Multiple journalist murders and disappearances in northern border states.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2006; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2006

2007.assessment:
Press freedom conditions deteriorating rapidly with drug war escalation. RSF
placed Mexico among most dangerous countries for journalists. Multiple journalists
killed or disappeared. Entire editorial staffs of some border newspapers forced
to flee. Cartels issuing explicit threats against media coverage. CPJ documented
significant impunity for crimes against journalists — few cases ever prosecuted.
National political coverage still reasonably free but severely constrained in
conflict zones.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2007; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2007

2008.assessment:
Worsening conditions. Multiple journalist murders (El Diario de Juarez, Milenio
correspondents, freelancers). Mexico entering list of world's most dangerous
countries for journalists alongside Iraq and Afghanistan. Newspapers in high-
violence areas forced to self-censor drug coverage or face lethal retaliation.
Cartels explicitly targeting journalist informants and those covering drug war.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2008; CPJ, Getting Away with Murder 2008

2009.assessment:
Near peak danger for journalists. Multiple killings in Ciudad Juarez, Chihuahua,
Guerrero. El Diario de Ciudad Juarez published front-page editorial addressing
cartels directly asking what rules to follow — ultimate symbol of press self-
censorship. RSF ranked Mexico around position 140-150 globally out of 175 countries.
Impunity for journalist crimes near total.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2009; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2009

2010.assessment:
Conditions still among world's worst for journalists. Multiple CPJ-confirmed
journalist murders. Zetas cartel particularly targeting journalists covering
organized crime in Tamaulipas. Social media (Twitter, blogs) filling void left
by self-censored traditional media in some violent areas — but bloggers also
targeted and killed. RSF around rank 136-150.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2010; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2010

2011.assessment:
Continued severe danger. Multiple journalist murders. Prominent cases: Maria
Elizabeth Macias Castro (Nuevo Laredo) found decapitated with note targeting
social media. Systematic impunity — nearly no prosecutions for journalist killings.
Mexico ranked among top 5-10 most dangerous countries for journalists globally.
Political press at national level retained more freedom but local reporting
severely constrained.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2011; CPJ, Attacks on the Press 2011

2012.assessment:
Slight improvement possible as Ciudad Juarez violence declined. RSF adjusted
methodology in 2013 to 0-100 scale. Pena Nieto government took office promising
security reform. Journalist murders continued — at least 7-8 journalists killed
in 2012 according to CPJ. Impunity near total. National political coverage more
open under new government media strategy but commercial media concentration growing.

sources.citation: RSF, Press Freedom Index 2012; CPJ Mexico country page 2012

2013.assessment:
RSF adopted new 0-100 scoring methodology in 2013. Mexico scored approximately
46-49 on new scale (higher=worse press conditions). Mexico ranked around 152/179.
Continued journalist murders. Pena Nieto government criticized by press freedom
organizations for surveillance of journalists and sources. Commercial media
consolidation (TV monopoly by Televisa/TV Azteca) limiting pluralism.
Very serious conditions.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2013, Mexico

2014.assessment:
Conditions broadly unchanged. Multiple journalist murders in 2014. Ayotzinapa
disappearances raised profile of state complicity in violence against civil society.
CPJ documented 5-7 journalist killings. Impunity continued — only 1-3% of journalist
murder cases result in conviction. Telecommunications reform did not address press
freedom concerns substantively. RSF rank around 150/180.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2014, Mexico

2015.assessment:
Conditions worsening again. RSF score approximately 50-52 on 0-100 bad scale.
Mexico ranked around 148/180. CJNG expanding violence to new regions, creating
new danger zones. Government surveillance program (Pegasus spyware) confirmed
targeting journalists and civil society — state-linked threats compounding
cartel threats. CPJ documented multiple journalist killings. Mechanism for
Protection of Journalists established but widely criticized as inadequate.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2015, Mexico; CPJ 2015

2016.assessment:
Mexico among world's worst for press freedom. RSF score approximately 52 (bad).
Rank 149/180. Multiple journalist murders, disappearances, forced exile. Pegasus
surveillance confirmed targeting journalists critical of government. Impunity
for journalist crimes near total. Some regions (Guerrero, Tamaulipas, Veracruz)
experienced near-total press self-censorship on sensitive topics.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2016, Mexico

2017.assessment:
2017 worst year on record for journalist killings in Mexico. At least 12 journalists
killed (RSF/CPJ counts). Mexico ranked 147/180 by RSF. Most dangerous year in recent
history, partly driven by 2018 election campaign-related violence. Pegasus spyware
targeting journalists confirmed by Citizen Lab. Impunity rate approximately 99%.
Some journalists self-exiling. Article 19 (press freedom NGO) documented record
violations.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2017, Mexico; Article 19 Mexico annual report 2017

2018.assessment:
Continued severe danger. Election year (AMLO won July 2018) with violence against
political reporters. Multiple journalist killings. AMLO during campaign pledged
to address journalist murders and surveillance. RSF ranked Mexico 147/180. CPJ
documented 9+ journalist killings. Impunity near total. Many journalists operating
under self-imposed constraints in cartel-controlled regions.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2018, Mexico; CPJ Mexico 2018

2019.assessment:
RSF score approximately 47-49. Mexico ranked 144/180 — some marginal improvement.
AMLO government initially maintained surveillance programs then claimed to end
Pegasus use. Journalist killings continuing at high rate. AMLO press conferences
(mananeras) created access but also used to publicly criticize and identify
critical journalists — "presidentializacion" of press hostility replacing
surveillance with public shaming. CPJ documented 10+ journalist killings.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2019, Mexico; CPJ 2019

2020.assessment:
Continuing severe conditions. RSF scored Mexico approximately 49-51, rank 143/180.
AMLO using daily press conferences to name and criticize journalists — creating
climate of hostility. Journalist murders continued throughout COVID year.
CPJ documented multiple killings. Some COVID-related censorship of reporters
covering hospital conditions. RSF and CPJ continued placing Mexico among global
worst 20 for press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2020, Mexico; Article 19 Mexico 2020

2021.assessment:
RSF gave Mexico score of approximately 51-53, rank 143/180. Conditions persistently
very serious. Article 19 documented 98 aggressions against journalists in first
half of 2021 alone. Multiple journalist murders. Cartel threats to local reporters
unabated. AMLO press conference attacks on specific journalists contributing to
environment of intimidation. Impunity for journalist crimes near total — few
cases ever investigated.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2021, Mexico; Article 19, Voces Vulnerables 2021

2022.assessment:
2022 among worst years. RSF score approximately 53-55, rank 127/180 (RSF adjusted
methodology again). At least 13 journalists killed in Mexico in 2022 — worst year
since records began. Murders concentrated in Veracruz, Guerrero, and other cartel-
controlled regions. AMLO continued public attacks on critical journalists. International
organizations (IDB, RSF, CPJ) raising alarm at record killings.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2022, Mexico; CPJ Mexico 2022

2023.assessment:
RSF 2023 score: approximately 40.14 (higher=worse on RSF scale), rank 128/180.
Conditions very serious. Mexico remains among world's most dangerous countries for
journalists. Journalist killings continued at high rate. Pegasus spyware use by
government agencies confirmed continuing by Citizen Lab. Impunity for journalist
murders near total. Local journalists in cartel-controlled areas face existential
threats. Pre-election period 2024 creating additional pressures.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2023, Mexico (score: 40.14)

2024.assessment:
RSF 2024: Mexico score approximately 39, rank 121/180 — marginal improvement in
ranking but still very serious. Sheinbaum won election June 2024. Multiple journalist
murders during 2024 election campaign. Sheinbaum as president signaled different
approach — less public hostility to critical press than AMLO. However cartel
threats to journalists unabated. CPJ documented multiple killings. Structural
impunity unchanged.

sources.citation: RSF, World Press Freedom Index 2024, Mexico

2025.assessment:
Press freedom conditions in 2025 unclear. RSF 2025 index not yet published.
Sheinbaum government broadly maintaining less overtly hostile approach to press
than AMLO. Cartel threats to journalists continuing. Structural impunity unchanged.
US-Mexico tensions (tariffs, migration) affecting media environment. 2025 RSF
data not yet available.

sources.citation: Article 19 Mexico monitoring 2025; CPJ

1995.assessment:
WB Statistical Capacity Indicator not available before 2004. Mexico's INEGI (National
Institute of Statistics, Geography, and Informatics) had operated since 1983 and was
a relatively capable statistical agency by Latin American standards. Population census
conducted regularly. GDP data produced. However, PRI-era political use of statistics
(Salinas government manipulation of 1988 election data) reduced confidence. Some
data quality and accessibility limitations. Estimated good capacity by regional
standards.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity database; INEGI institutional history

1996.assessment:
INEGI continuing to produce regular statistical outputs. National accounts, price
indices, employment surveys regularly published. Some improvement in methodology
following Tequila crisis when better economic data was needed for IMF programs.
Data accessibility limited by pre-internet distribution. Good by Latin American
standards but below OECD norms.

sources.citation: INEGI institutional reports; IMF Article IV Mexico 1996

1997.assessment:
Statistical capacity stable. INEGI producing regular data series. Post-Tequila
crisis IMF engagement improved data standards somewhat. Internet beginning to
enable wider data distribution. Mexico's statistical system well-developed
relative to region. Household survey (ENIGH) conducted periodically providing
poverty and inequality data.

sources.citation: INEGI; IMF statistical standards assessments

1998.assessment:
Gradual improvement in statistical transparency as internet allowed wider data
distribution. INEGI website emerging as data access portal. Mexico's SNA (System
of National Accounts) aligned with international standards. Some gaps in sub-national
data and administrative statistics.

sources.citation: INEGI annual report; World Bank Mexico country data

1999.assessment:
Statistical capacity continuing to improve. INEGI strengthening methodology
ahead of 2000 census. Internet distribution of data expanding. Pre-democratic
transition period with INEGI maintaining institutional continuity. Estimates
approaching WB SCI first measurement of approximately 70-72 in 2004.

sources.citation: INEGI; estimated from WB SCI trajectory

2000.assessment:
2000 population and housing census conducted — major statistical undertaking.
Democratic transition with Fox government committed to statistical transparency.
INEGI formally given autonomous status in law (Ley del INEGI eventually passed
2006). Internet-based data access expanding significantly. WB SCI not yet available
but estimated at approximately 70.

sources.citation: INEGI, Censo de Poblacion y Vivienda 2000; estimated from WB SCI

2001.assessment:
Statistical capacity broadly maintained. Fox government expanding data accessibility.
INEGI website improving. Transparency Law (LFTAIPG, passed 2002) creating framework
for statistical data access. Employment and labor market surveys regular. National
accounts timely.

sources.citation: INEGI; estimated

2002.assessment:
Transparency Law (LFTAIPG 2002) improving data access. INEGI data portal expanding.
ENIGH household survey regular. Mexico disseminating data to IMF and World Bank
on standard schedules. Statistical capacity improving gradually.

sources.citation: INEGI; estimated

2003.assessment:
Statistical capacity stable and gradually improving. INEGI producing regular
output across all standard domains. Data accessible online. Mexico meeting IMF
data standards (SDDS subscription). Approaching WB SCI first measurement
period of approximately 71-72.

sources.citation: IMF Mexico SDDS; INEGI

2004.assessment:
First WB SCI measurement for Mexico: approximately 71. Good statistical capacity
by developing country standards. INEGI producing regular national accounts, price
data, employment surveys, household surveys, trade statistics. Data accessible
online. Some gaps in subnational data, municipal-level statistics, and real-time
data publication.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2004

2005.assessment:
Improving statistical capacity. INEGI data portal expanding. ENIGH household
survey conducted. Mexico disseminating economic data on standard IMF schedules.
Gradual improvement in timeliness of statistical releases.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2005

2006.assessment:
Ley del INEGI passed in 2006 giving INEGI formal legal autonomy — major
improvement for statistical independence. INEGI no longer fully under executive
control. This reinforced statistical credibility. Calderon took office December
2006. INEGI beginning new survey designs. WB SCI approximately 73.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2006; Ley del INEGI 2006

2007.assessment:
INEGI operating with greater autonomy under new law. Statistical production quality
improving. New methodologies being implemented. ENIGH surveys more frequent.
Labor market statistics improving. National accounts revisions bringing closer
alignment with international standards.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2007

2008.assessment:
INEGI continuing improvements. Financial crisis created demand for more granular
economic data. INEGI released new GDP methodology. New economic censuses underway.
Statistical capacity steadily improving from 71 baseline to mid-70s range.
Mexico among better statistical agencies in Latin America.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2008

2009.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained during crisis year. INEGI tracking economic
contraction. GDP and labor market data regular and credible. Drug war-related
statistics (homicide data) becoming increasingly important but contentious —
INEGI and SNSP (National Security System) sometimes providing different counts.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2009

2010.assessment:
2010 population and housing census (Censo de Poblacion y Vivienda 2010) conducted
successfully. INEGI capacity demonstrated across full statistical lifecycle.
Data accessible through improved online portals. Mexico's statistical system
well-regarded internationally. WB SCI approximately 76.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2010; INEGI Censo 2010

2011.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained. New data dissemination systems. Conteo
de poblacion intercensal surveys. Crime statistics debates continuing between
INEGI victimization surveys (ENVIPE) and government crime reports (SNSP) —
significant difference indicating dark figure of crime. Both series published.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2011

2012.assessment:
Statistical capacity at solid level. INEGI increasingly open data portal.
Mexico joined Open Government Partnership (OGP) in 2011, enhancing data
openness commitments. Pena Nieto government continuing INEGI autonomy.
Economic census 2009 results fully published. New labor market data improvements.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2012

2013.assessment:
Good statistical capacity. INEGI datos.gob.mx open data portal launched.
Mexico pushing for open data standards across government agencies. Statistical
capacity broadly at 77 in WB SCI. GDP revisions published transparently.
ENIGH poverty data regular. Financial sector statistics through Banxico high quality.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2013

2014.assessment:
Statistical capacity continuing gradual improvement. INEGI autonomous and well-
resourced. datos.gob.mx open data portal expanding. Inter-census population count
(Encuesta Intercensal 2015) being prepared. Energy reform required new oil sector
statistics. Overall good capacity maintained.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator, Mexico 2014

2015.assessment:
Encuesta Intercensal 2015 (large mid-census survey of 6 million households)
